nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving to still shallow freshwaters of temperate East Asia — ponds, lakes, and stream margins, typically on fine sandy or silty substrates with adequate light penetration.

decorPreferences: Fine sand or fine nutrient-rich gravel substrate
Open background space for runner spread and vertical leaf rise
Root tabs near the crown for heavy root feeders
Sufficient tank depth to display the spiraled ribbon foliage

summary: The Contortion Giant Corkscrew Vallisneria is a distinctive freshwater background plant (Vallisneria asiatica) known for its tightly spiraled, light-green ribbon leaves. Generally considered easy to keep and beginner-friendly, it spreads readily via runners.
careNotes: Contortion Giant Corkscrew Vallisneria is most visually distinctive among the commonly traded Vallisneria for its tightly twisted, corkscrew-spiraled ribbon leaves — a feature that sets it apart from the straight-leaved V. spiralis (fw-plant-008) and the much taller Jungle Val (fw-plant-034). It is generally regarded as a beginner-friendly background plant that tolerates a wide range of community-tank parameters. As with all Vallisneria, it is commonly advised to plant the crown JUST ABOVE the substrate — burying the crown is a frequent cause of rot. Once established, it spreads aggressively by stolons (runners that grow along the substrate, producing daughter plants as they go); in most aquaria this requires periodic thinning to keep the bed from overtaking the layout. CO2 is not required, but growth rate and coloration tend to improve noticeably with supplementation. Vallisneria is a heavy root feeder, so root tabs placed near the crown generally outperform water-column liquid fertilizer alone. It has been reported to be sensitive to liquid carbon products (Excel-type glutaraldehyde), which can cause leaf melt. The species' taxonomic status is debated — it is sometimes treated as a synonym of V. spiralis or as a variety under the name V. spiralis var. tortifolia, and trade names overlap heavily between the two. The spiraled-leaf morphology remains the clearest distinguishing feature in the aquarium trade. The plant is native to Japan.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Confirmed scientific name (Vallisneria asiatica), Japanese native range, distinctive spiraled-leaf morphology, background placement, temperature 70-82°F (21-28°C), moderate-to-fast growth, easy care difficulty, propagation via runners, CO2 not required, nutrient-rich substrate preference.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed background placement, easy care difficulty, fast runner-based propagation (new shoot as often as every 3 days), tall growth that can drape across the surface, and compatibility with goldfish/African cichlid/community tanks. Did not provide specific numeric water parameters; high-stakes numeric fields therefore aligned with sister species V. spiralis (fw-plant-008) pending reviewer confirmation.
